_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: A yield curve plots Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with a normal upward slope indicating higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting term and risk premiums. An inverted yield curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded all modern US recessions by 6-18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield sits at 4.38% for June 2026, and the 10-year minus 2-year (2s10s) spread is positive at 0.28 points. This is a notable shift from the deepest inversion of -0.2 points during 2022-2024, indicating the curve has re-steepened above zero.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread serves as a key recession indicator because inversion has historically signaled economic slowdowns. The yield curve spent 2022-2024 inverted but has since re-steepened to a positive 0.28 points, suggesting the immediate recession signal from inversion has weakened. Nevertheless, the New York Fed assigns a 31% probability to a recession, indicating moderate risk remains. Importantly, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession begins, so a positive slope does not guarantee recession avoidance.
notes.now: Taken together, the positive 10Y-2Y spread after a period of inversion and a 31% recession probability signal ongoing economic caution but not certainty. The yield curve is currently not inverted, reducing one traditional warning sign, yet recession risk remains elevated according to the NY Fed indicator.
